questions: Identify the long-running online television episode guide that has been cataloging TV series since 1995 and contains episode lists for over 12,400 shows. After identifying it, describe its core functionality and features, including: how it structures episode information, how it connects users to expanded episode details, what options it provides for simplified printable summaries, how it presents current broadcast schedules across multiple countries, and what additional related resources or tools (such as data downloads, apps, or merchandise links) it offers to users.

A long-running online television episode catalog states that while it lists only episode titles and airdates, most titles link out to external databases that provide richer details. It also instructs users to send corrections and updates to the editors of those same external platforms when visiting them. Identify the two third-party television databases referenced for expanded episode information and corrections. For each one, explain (a) the types of additional episode details it is known for providing beyond basic titles and airdates, and (b) how users can typically submit or contribute corrections or updates on that platform.

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging episodic information since 1995 and focuses on listing episode titles and original airdates for thousands of series. Describe the scope of its database, how it supplements its basic episode lists through partnerships or external links, the special viewing or summary features it offers for individual series, the types of schedule grids and regional listings it provides, any downloadable data formats mentioned, and the process it directs users to follow for corrections or updates.

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging episode lists since the mid-1990s and now indexes more than 12,000 TV series. After identifying it, describe its core features, including: how its episode lists are structured and what happens when a user clicks an episode title; how users can access full-episode summaries in a simplified format; what types of current schedule grids and regional listings it provides; what download or data-export options are available; and how users are instructed to submit corrections or updates to episode information. Provide a comprehensive overview of these elements.

Identify the long-running online television episode guide that has been cataloging TV series since 1995 and contains episode lists for more than 12,000 shows. Then, provide a comprehensive overview of its key features and navigation options. Your answer should describe: how episode information is structured and enhanced through external links; how users can access full-episode summaries in an alternate format; what types of schedule grids and regional listings are available; what downloadable data formats are offered; and at least three additional resources or related sections accessible from its main navigation (such as themed media, apps, merchandise, related sites, radio listings, or FAQs).

Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode lists since 1995 and now covers more than 12,000 shows. After identifying the site, provide a comprehensive overview of its key features, including: its stated mission or tagline; the approximate number of shows it tracks; how it handles episode titles and where those links typically lead for expanded information; how users can access full-episode summaries in a simplified format; what types of schedule grids it offers (including regions); what options it provides for exporting show data; how users can submit corrections; and at least three additional resources or sections highlighted in its main navigation (such as themed merchandise, related media, or radio listings).

The long-running online television episode guide is **epguides.com**. It has been cataloging TV series since 1995 and describes itself as carrying episode lists for more than 12,400 shows, making it one of the web’s oldest continuously maintained TV episode indexes [1]. Its core function is to organize television series by show and then list episodes in a structured tabular format, typically including season and episode numbers, titles, original air dates, and often production or special numbering details for quick reference [2].

Each show page also links users to expanded information beyond the main list. epguides.com connects visitors to external episode-detail resources such as TVMaze, IMDb, and The Futon Critic, allowing users to move from a concise guide to richer cast, plot, and production data [2]. For users who want cleaner output, the site offers **printer-friendly** or simplified summary versions of episode guides, making it easier to save or print compact listings without heavier page formatting [2].

The site also provides a **current schedules** section that tracks what is airing now, with listings presented for multiple countries, including the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, and Ireland [3]. Beyond episode guides themselves, epguides.com offers related utilities and resources such as downloadable data files, mobile/app links, and merchandise or shopping links tied to television content and guide use, extending it from a static index into a broader TV-reference hub [1][2].

## References
[1] epguides.com, homepage/about information, including site history and show count: https://epguides.com/  
[2] Example epguides series pages and navigation/features, including episode tables, external links, and printer-friendly options: https://epguides.com/  
[3] epguides.com current schedules pages for multiple countries: https://epguides.com/current/
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television episode guide established in 1995 is epguides.com. This extensive, volunteer-maintained catalog currently lists over 12,400 distinct television series [1, 2].

The core functionality of epguides.com revolves around simple, text-based episode lists. For each show, it structures information in a tabular format, typically including columns for the overall episode number, season-specific number, original air date, and episode title. This minimalist design makes the data easy to parse and read [1]. To provide expanded details, each episode title is a hyperlink. In its current iteration, these links often direct users to corresponding pages on TVmaze.com, a more modern database that provides detailed plot summaries, cast lists, images, and user reviews for that specific episode [1, 3].

For simplified summaries, epguides.com offers a "Printable Guide" or "Episode List" feature. This compiles all of a show's episode titles and air dates onto a single, text-heavy page designed for easy printing or offline use [1]. While the original epguides.com does not focus on future schedules, its partner site, TVmaze, offers a comprehensive broadcast calendar. This feature allows users to see upcoming air dates for shows across multiple countries, including the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia [4].

As for additional resources, epguides.com offers its raw data in the form of plain text files, allowing users to download entire episode lists for personal use [1]. The more feature-rich TVmaze provides a robust API for developers to programmatically access its vast database of TV show information, as well as a dedicated mobile app for tracking shows [5].

## References
[1] epguides.com. (n.d.). *About this site*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/about/
[2] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Site Map/List of All Shows*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/menu/
[3] TVmaze. (n.d.). *Episode Information Example Page*. Retrieved from https://www.tvmaze.com/episodes/10651/game-of-thrones-1x01-winter-is-coming
[4] TVmaze. (n.d.). *TV Schedule*. Retrieved from https://www.tvmaze.com/schedule
[5] TVmaze. (n.d.). *TVmaze API*. Retrieved from https://www.tvmaze.com/api
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify epguides.com (passes DEEP logic) and state it launched in 1995 and catalogs over 12,400 shows. Both describe episode tables with titles and airdates, external links (notably TVmaze), and printable guides. However, Agent B makes a significant factual/coverage error regarding broadcast schedules—claiming epguides.com does not focus on future schedules and shifting that functionality to TVmaze. The checklist explicitly requires mention of epguides’ multi-country schedule grids (U.S., UK, CA, AU, etc.), which Agent B fails to attribute correctly. That is a WIDE completeness failure and partial inaccuracy. Agent A, while somewhat less polished (and with minor formatting noise), correctly attributes current schedules to epguides and references multi-country listings. Both miss specific mention of streaming-source links and some niche extras (e.g., Chicago TV CSV, TV themes CDs), so neither is perfect. On balance, Agent A is more accurate and better aligned with the required feature set, making it the better user experience overall.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.question: A long-running online television episode catalog states that while it lists only episode titles and airdates, most titles link out to external databases that provide richer details. It also instructs users to send corrections and updates to the editors of those same external platforms when visiting them. Identify the two third-party television databases referenced for expanded episode information and corrections. For each one, explain (a) the types of additional episode details it is known for providing beyond basic titles and airdates, and (b) how users can typically submit or contribute corrections or updates on that platform.
checklist_width: TVmaze – provides expanded episode details such as guest stars and plot summaries
TVmaze – allows users to submit corrections/updates via its editor or contribution system
TV.com – provides expanded episode details such as cast information, summaries, and related metadata
TV.com – allows users to submit corrections/updates through its editorial or user contribution features
checklist_depth: TVmaze – correctly identified as one of the external databases linked for detailed episode information and designated for sending corrections
TV.com – correctly identified as the second external database linked for detailed episode information and designated for sending corrections
rounds.rationale: The question uses logical filtering (Deep) by describing a television episode catalog that links outward for richer data and directs corrections to those same destinations, without naming the catalog itself. This forces identification of the two specific third-party databases that fit both conditions. It then requires broad aggregation (Wide) by asking for distinct details about each platform’s content offerings and contribution mechanisms, ensuring the respondent must research and synthesize information about both entities rather than relying on a single source.

The two third-party databases are **TVMaze** and **IMDb**. epguides explains that its own pages mainly provide episode titles and original airdates, while many episode-title links send users to those outside databases for fuller information; it also tells visitors to submit corrections or updates directly to the editors of those sites rather than to epguides itself [1].

**TVMaze** is known for expanded television metadata such as episode summaries, season and episode numbering, runtime, images, cast/guest-cast associations, network/platform details, and links tying episodes to broader series information [2]. For corrections, TVMaze typically relies on user participation through its site tools and community workflow: registered users can edit eligible show data or submit update requests/report issues through the platform’s contribution and support mechanisms, where changes are reviewed within the site’s editorial system [2][3].

**IMDb** provides richer detail beyond title and airdate through episode pages that commonly include plot summaries, full cast and crew, user ratings, trivia, parental guidance, release information, photos, and sometimes technical credits and keywords [4]. Corrections on IMDb are usually submitted through its **“Edit page”** or related contribution interfaces. Users sign in, choose the affected title, and then add or revise fields such as cast, plot, release dates, runtimes, or episode metadata; submissions are processed through IMDb’s contribution review pipeline and associated help guidance [4][5].

So, the referenced pair is TVMaze and IMDb: TVMaze emphasizes structured TV-episode reference data and show relationships, while IMDb adds broader production, credit, and community-generated detail; both accept user-supplied corrections through their own contribution systems rather than through epguides [1][2][4].

## References
[1] epguides.com FAQ / help text describing links to outside databases and directing corrections to those editors: https://epguides.com/faq.html  
[2] TVMaze, episode/show pages and API/documentation illustrating available episode metadata: https://www.tvmaze.com/ and https://www.tvmaze.com/api  
[3] TVMaze support/contribution-related pages: https://www.tvmaze.com/faq  
[4] IMDb Help Center and title/episode pages describing episode details and contribution features: https://help.imdb.com/ and https://www.imdb.com/  
[5] IMDb contribution guidance (“Edit page” / adding or correcting data): https://help.imdb.com/article/contribution/contribution-information/contribution-basics/GQCBN3BTPKTDBJMH
rounds.answer_b:
The two third-party television databases referenced for expanded episode information and corrections are TVmaze.com and TheTVDB.com [1]. These platforms serve as comprehensive, community-edited resources that supplement the basic data provided by minimalist episode guides.

TVmaze.com is known for providing a wealth of supplementary details for each episode. Beyond titles and airdates, it offers detailed plot summaries, runtimes, lists of main and guest cast members, and specific crew credits for roles like director and writer. The platform also includes an extensive gallery of episode-specific images, user ratings, and a comments section for discussion [2]. To contribute corrections, users must first create an account. Once registered, they can use the "Edit" buttons located on show and episode pages to submit changes or add missing information. These submissions are then reviewed and approved by site moderators to ensure accuracy [3].

TheTVDB.com (The TV Database) also offers rich episode details, including plot overviews, writer and director credits, and production codes. A key feature of TheTVDB is its extensive support for international audiences, providing episode titles and summaries in multiple languages. It is particularly valued for its vast repository of high-quality, user-contributed artwork, such as posters, fan art, and episode-specific images [4]. The contribution process is similar to TVmaze's. Users must register for an account to participate. They can then click "Edit" on a series or episode page to submit corrections or add new data. All user-submitted changes are placed into a moderation queue to be verified by site editors before becoming public [5].

## References
[1] epguides.com. (n.d.). *About This Site*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/about/
[2] TVmaze. (n.d.). *Queen Charlotte: A Bridgerton Story - 1x01: Queen to Be*. Retrieved from https://www.tvmaze.com/episodes/2332152/queen-charlotte-a-bridgerton-story-1x01-queen-to-be
[3] TVmaze. (n.d.). *Contribution Guidelines*. Retrieved from https://www.tvmaze.com/faq/2/contribute
[4] TheTVDB.com. (n.d.). *Game of Thrones - 1x1: Winter Is Coming*. Retrieved from https://www.thetvdb.com/series/game-of-thrones/episodes/325462
[5] TheTVDB.com. (n.d.). *Contribution Rules & Guidelines*. Retrieved from https://support.thetvdb.com/kb/faq.php?id=3
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: The correct pair is TVmaze and TV.com. Agent A identified TVMaze and IMDb (incorrect second entity). Agent B identified TVmaze and TheTVDB.com (incorrect second entity). Therefore, BOTH agents failed the core entity requirement (DEEP failure).

Next, Width/Completeness: For the databases they chose, both agents provided accurate and reasonably detailed explanations of episode metadata and contribution mechanisms. However, because each substituted the wrong second database, they failed the WIDTH checklist tied to TV.com.

Since both agents missed a required core entity (TV.com), this is a Low Quality Tie. Even though formatting and explanations were solid in both responses, the foundational identification error overrides presentation advantages.

Mentions the 'Printable Guide' option for episode summaries
Notes that streaming video source links are listed at the bottom of each show's page

rounds.answer_a:
The site is **epguides.com**, a long-running online TV reference created by Bruce A. Davis that has cataloged episodic information since **1995**. Its core purpose is to provide concise episode guides—primarily **episode titles and original airdates**—for **thousands of television series**, including current, ended, and syndicated shows.[1][2]

Its database is intentionally minimalist, but it supplements those basic lists in several ways. epguides notes that many pages include links to richer external resources, especially through its long association with **TVmaze**, whose pages can add fuller cast, character, and production details beyond epguides’ stripped-down guide format.[1][3] For individual series, the site also offers special viewing tools such as **summaries, recaps, and episode-number navigation**, and some pages provide alternative list views or sorting to help track runs and seasons.[1][2]

Beyond series pages, epguides supplies multiple schedule resources: **primetime grids**, **cable listings**, and other **regional or network-based schedules**, helping users browse what aired and when across different markets or channels.[1] It also mentions downloadable guide data in plain-text style formats suitable for offline use or import, including **text/tabular data files** on some sections of the site.[1][2]

For corrections or updates, epguides directs users to use its **submission/update instructions**—typically by contacting the maintainer through the site’s designated feedback or email pathway and providing the series name, episode information, and the proposed correction so it can be verified and incorporated.[1][2]

## References
[1] epguides.com, site home/about/update information and navigation pages, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/  
[2] epguides.com, FAQ/help/update-related pages and individual series guide structure, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/  
[3] TVmaze, reference pages linked from epguides for supplemental television series information, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.tvmaze.com/
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging episodic information since 1995 is epguides.com [1]. Its database focuses on providing episode lists with titles and original airdates for thousands of TV series, primarily from English-speaking countries [1, 2].

The site supplements its core data through external links on each show's page, directing users to more comprehensive resources like IMDb, TVmaze, TheTVDB.com, and Wikipedia for cast details, plot summaries, and user reviews [5]. For individual series, epguides.com offers several special viewing features, including a "compact" view, a "one-page" summary format, a "printable" list, and a "shareable" list [5]. It also links to calendars for tracking season premieres and finales [3].

The site provides several schedule grids and regional listings, including Primetime grids for major US networks (ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox, CW), as well as national schedules for the UK, Canada, and Australia [3]. While it doesn't offer complex downloadable formats, all show lists are available as a tab-delimited text file via a ".txt" link, allowing users to import the data into spreadsheets [4].

For corrections or additions, the site's FAQ directs users to email the editor directly. Submissions must be accompanied by verifiable information from a reliable source; unsourced claims are not accepted. The site explicitly states it is not a wiki and is maintained by a site editor [2].

## References
[1] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Home Page*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/
[2] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/
[3] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Schedules*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/grid/
[4] epguides.com. (n.d.). *FAQ: Can I download your data?*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/#download
[5] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Example Series Page (Doctor Who)*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/DoctorWho_2005/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify the site as epguides.com and describe its founding in 1995 and focus on concise episode title/airdate listings. Both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both fail to include many required checklist items. Neither mentions the specific figure of over 12,400 shows. Neither clearly states that episode titles link specifically to TVmaze for added details as described in the checklist (B lists multiple external sites without matching the stated mechanism). Neither mentions streaming video source links at the bottom of show pages, the Chicago TV shows .csv download, UK Radio Shows listings, or the full set of regional grids (US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, Korea). The correction process via TVmaze or TV.com is also not accurately described by either. Both therefore significantly fail the WIDE aggregation requirement.

Presentation & UX: Agent A uses slightly clearer structure and bolding, while Agent B provides somewhat more concrete feature examples and citations. However, both omit major required facts and include minor inaccuracies (e.g., B’s downloadable format description).

Because both agents get the main entity correct but miss multiple critical checklist elements, this is a LOW quality tie driven by incomplete coverage (WIDE failure), not deep logic errors.

rounds.answer_a:
The site is **epguides.com**, the long-running TV reference created by Bruce A. Davis. It has cataloged television episode information since **1995** and now indexes **more than 12,000 TV series**.[1] Its main function is to present compact episode guides listing **episode numbers, titles, and original air dates** in a simple season-by-season layout. On many series pages, clicking an **episode title** takes the user to additional information, typically a fuller summary or an external detail page when available.[1][2]

For users who want fuller descriptions without a cluttered interface, epguides also offers access to **full-episode summaries in a simplified “brief” or stripped-down text style**, making plot recaps easier to read or print.[2] The site remains intentionally minimal, but it supplements its guides through linked resources and summary pages.[1][2]

Its scheduling tools include several **current TV schedule grids**, such as **primetime network grids**, plus **cable and regional listings**, helping users browse what is airing across U.S. broadcast and other channel lineups.[1] epguides also provides **downloadable data options**, including **plain-text and delimited/tabular files** for some guide information, useful for offline reference or import into personal databases and spreadsheets.[1][3]

For corrections or updates, the site instructs users to follow its **update/submission guidance** by sending the relevant series name, episode details, and corrected information to the maintainer through the site’s designated contact method so the data can be reviewed and amended.[1][2]

## References
[1] epguides.com, home page and site navigation/about/update information, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/  
[2] epguides.com, FAQ/help and individual series guide pages, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/  
[3] epguides.com, downloadable data/export-related pages, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging episode lists since the mid-1990s is epguides.com, which now indexes over 12,000 series [1]. Its core feature is the episode list, structured as a table with columns for episode number, production code, original airdate, and title. When a user clicks on an individual episode title, they are redirected to a new tab showing that specific episode's page on a partner site like TVmaze, which provides detailed plot summaries, cast lists, and other information [2, 5].

For a simplified overview, each series page on epguides.com offers special viewing options, including a "one page" summary format that condenses the entire episode list into a single, continuous page for easy viewing or printing [5]. The site provides several current schedule grids, including a Primetime grid for major U.S. networks (ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox, CW) and national schedules for the UK, Canada, and Australia [3].

For data export, every show list is available for download as a tab-delimited text file by clicking a ".txt" link on the series page, allowing the data to be easily imported into spreadsheet applications [4]. The site specifies it is not a wiki; users are instructed to submit corrections or updates by emailing the editor directly. All submissions must include a verifiable source, as unsubstantiated claims are not accepted [2].

## References
[1] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Home Page*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/
[2] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/
[3] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Schedules*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/grid/
[4] epguides.com. (n.d.). *FAQ: Can I download your data?* Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/#download
[5] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Example Series Page (Doctor Who)*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/DoctorWho_2005/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the site as epguides.com and acknowledge its mid-1990s origins and 12,000+ show index, so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, on WIDTH/completeness, Agent A is more vague and partially inaccurate. A does not clearly state that episode titles link specifically to TVmaze, gives an imprecise description of the printable/"Printable Guide" format, provides only general statements about schedule grids without naming specific grids or regions, and incorrectly states that corrections are sent to the site maintainer directly rather than via TVmaze/TV.com as instructed. Agent B more clearly explains the episode table structure, explicitly mentions redirection to TVmaze, describes the condensed viewing format, and gives more concrete examples of schedule grids and export options. While B also misses some specific regional listings and slightly misstates the correction workflow, it is more specific, better grounded, and more helpful overall. Therefore, B wins on completeness and user experience, though not by a wide margin.

rounds.question: Identify the long-running online television episode guide that has been cataloging TV series since 1995 and contains episode lists for more than 12,000 shows. Then, provide a comprehensive overview of its key features and navigation options. Your answer should describe: how episode information is structured and enhanced through external links; how users can access full-episode summaries in an alternate format; what types of schedule grids and regional listings are available; what downloadable data formats are offered; and at least three additional resources or related sections accessible from its main navigation (such as themed media, apps, merchandise, related sites, radio listings, or FAQs).
checklist_width: States that the site has cataloged TV shows since 1995
Mentions that it contains episode lists for over 12,400 shows
Explains that episode lists show titles and airdates with links to TVmaze for more details
Describes the 'Printable Guide' option for full episode summaries
Identifies schedule grids (e.g., This Week’s U.S. Schedule Grid, Fall 2025 Grid, regional grids such as US/UK/CA/AU/NZ/JP/KR)
Mentions availability of downloadable data such as Chicago TV listings in .csv format
References UK Radio Shows listings
Lists at least three additional resources (e.g., TV themes on CD, apps, TV-related merchants, other TV-related websites, FAQ, customizable TV listings, acknowledgments/disclaimer)
checklist_depth: epguides.com identified as the website + proof it has cataloged TV episodes since 1995
epguides.com identified as hosting over 12,400 TV show episode lists with title/airdate format and TVmaze links
rounds.rationale: The question uses temporal (since 1995), quantitative (12,000+ shows), and structural clues (title/airdate lists with TVmaze links and printable guides) to logically identify the specific website without naming it directly (Deep). It then requires aggregating numerous distinct navigation features, tools, formats, and related resources from across the site’s main menu, ensuring broad information synthesis rather than a single fact lookup (Wide).

rounds.answer_a:
The site is **epguides.com**, a long-running online TV episode-guide reference created by Bruce A. Davis that has cataloged television series since **1995** and now indexes **more than 12,000 shows**.[1] Its core episode guides are deliberately compact, usually organized by **season and episode number**, with each entry listing the **title** and **original airdate**. Those basic lists are often enhanced by outbound links: clicking an episode title can lead to extra detail pages or connected resources, and series pages may link to external databases such as **TVmaze** for fuller cast, character, and production information.[1][2]

For viewers who want plot information in a cleaner presentation, epguides offers access to **full-episode summaries in an alternate simplified format**, giving recap-style content in a more stripped-down, easy-to-read view.[1][2] Beyond episode pages, the site provides **current schedule tools**, including **primetime network grids**, **cable schedule grids**, and **regional listings** for different markets or viewing areas.[1]

The site also mentions downloadable guide data, including **plain-text** and **tabular/delimited data formats**, intended for offline reference or import into personal databases and spreadsheets.[1][3]

Its main navigation extends beyond episode guides. Additional resources include **TV theme music/media sections**, **mobile or app-related access points**, **merchandise/store links**, **related television-reference sites**, **radio listings**, and a **FAQ/help area** explaining usage and updates.[1][2] For corrections, users are directed to follow the site’s update instructions by sending the relevant series, episode details, and proposed changes through the maintainer’s designated contact method for review.[1][2]

## References
[1] epguides.com, home page and navigation resources, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/  
[2] epguides.com, FAQ/help and individual series guide pages, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/  
[3] epguides.com, data/download-related pages, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television episode guide that has been active since 1995 is epguides.com, which now contains episode lists for more than 12,000 shows [1]. Episode information on the site is structured in a clear tabular format, displaying the season/episode number, production code, original airdate, and title for each installment. This core data is enhanced through external links; clicking on an individual episode title directs users to a partner site like TVmaze for full plot summaries, cast lists, and reviews [5]. Each series page also links to other major databases like IMDb and TheTVDB to provide more comprehensive information [5].

For an alternative viewing experience of the episode list, users can access an entire series' data in a condensed "one page" summary format, which is ideal for printing or quick reference [5]. The site provides several schedule grids and regional listings, most notably a Primetime schedule for the five major US networks (ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox, CW), along with national schedules for the UK, Canada, and Australia [3]. Regarding data export, all show lists are offered as a downloadable tab-delimited text file via a ".txt" link on the series page, allowing the data to be used in spreadsheets [4]. Beyond its core database, key features accessible from the main navigation include a detailed FAQ section covering correction submissions, a calendar for tracking season premieres and finales, and a section dedicated to Radio Listings [1, 2, 3].

## References
[1] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Home Page*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/
[2] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/
[3] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Schedules*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/grid/
[4] epguides.com. (n.d.). *FAQ: Can I download your data?* Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/#download
[5] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Example Series Page (Doctor Who)*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/DoctorWho_2005/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify epguides.com as the site launched in 1995 and hosting over 12,000 episode guides, so both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Both cover episode structure (titles + airdates + external TVmaze links), alternate/printable summary formats, schedule grids, downloadable data, radio listings, and at least three additional resources. However, both miss some highly specific checklist items (e.g., Chicago .csv listings, explicit UK Radio Shows labeling, and certain named regional grids). Neither commits major hallucinations. Agent A is slightly more comprehensive in describing additional navigation resources (themes, apps, merchandise, related sites, FAQ, radio), while Agent B is somewhat more specific about file formats (.txt) but less expansive overall.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A clearly wins on formatting and scannability. It uses bolding, cleaner structure, and more logically grouped sections, making it easier to navigate quickly. Agent B is more wall-of-text and less visually structured. Although B has more citations, the difference in readability and structured presentation favors A.

Conclusion: Both are accurate, but Agent A provides a better user experience through clearer structure and slightly broader coverage of navigation features. Therefore, A_BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: Identify the long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episode lists since 1995 and now covers more than 12,000 shows. After identifying the site, provide a comprehensive overview of its key features, including: its stated mission or tagline; the approximate number of shows it tracks; how it handles episode titles and where those links typically lead for expanded information; how users can access full-episode summaries in a simplified format; what types of schedule grids it offers (including regions); what options it provides for exporting show data; how users can submit corrections; and at least three additional resources or sections highlighted in its main navigation (such as themed merchandise, related media, or radio listings).
checklist_width: Site tagline/mission statement about cataloging TV since 1995
Approximate number of shows covered (12,400+)
Explanation that episode lists show titles and airdates, with titles linking to TVmaze for more details
Description of the 'Printable Guide' option for full episode summaries
Mention of U.S. schedule grid plus other regions (UK, CA, AU, NZ, JP, KR)
Reference to export option such as shows available as .csv (e.g., Chicago TV all shows)
Explanation of how corrections are submitted (via editors at TVmaze or TV.com)
At least three additional navigation/resources (e.g., TV themes on CD, apps, TV-related merchants, radio shows, FAQ, customizable listings)
checklist_depth: Identification of the site as epguides.com + proof via founding year (1995), scope (12,400+ shows), and its distinctive linking structure to TVmaze
rounds.rationale: The question uses identifying clues (founding year, number of shows, linking to TVmaze, printable guides, and multi-region schedule grids) to logically narrow down the correct website without naming it (Deep reasoning). It then requires the respondent to aggregate numerous distinct features—navigation sections, correction process, export formats, and regional grids—spread across the site’s main interface (Wide aggregation). This ensures both correct identification and comprehensive information synthesis.

rounds.answer_a:
The site is **epguides.com**, Bruce A. Davis’s long-running TV reference that says it has been “cataloging the TV universe” since **1995** and now tracks **more than 12,000 shows**.[1] Its core mission is to provide compact episode guides centered on the essentials: episode number, title, and original airdate, usually arranged season by season for quick lookup.[1][2]

On individual series pages, **episode titles are clickable**. Those links typically open expanded information through partner or related resources—most notably **TVmaze** pages—or other detail pages when available, giving users access to fuller cast, crew, and synopsis material beyond epguides’ minimalist lists.[1][3] For users who want plot detail without a heavy interface, epguides also provides access to **full-episode summaries in a simpler, stripped-down format**, designed for easier reading and printing.[2]

Its schedule tools go beyond series indexes. The site offers **current TV schedule grids**, including **broadcast primetime grids**, **cable grids**, and **regional listings** for **U.S., Canadian, and U.K.** viewing contexts.[1] For users who want offline use or database import, epguides provides **export/download options** such as **CSV**, **tab-delimited**, and **plain-text/text-only** formats for show data.[1][2]

For corrections or updates, epguides directs users to use its **Submit Corrections** / update instructions, generally by contacting the maintainer with the series name, episode details, and the proposed fix so the information can be reviewed and incorporated.[1][2]

Its main navigation also highlights several related resources, including **theme music/media links**, **TV and radio schedule sections**, **merchandise/store links**, **related/reference sites**, and a **FAQ/help** area explaining formats, links, and update procedures.[1][2]

## References
[1] epguides.com, home page and main navigation, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/  
[2] epguides.com, FAQ/help and submission/update information, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.epguides.com/faq/  
[3] TVmaze, linked supplemental series information referenced from epguides pages, accessed April 11, 2026. https://www.tvmaze.com/
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television reference site that has been cataloging TV episodes since 1995 is epguides.com [1]. Operating with the simple tagline "A TV Episode Guide," the site currently tracks over 12,100 television series [1]. Its primary feature is providing clear episode lists structured in tables that show episode numbers, production codes, original airdates, and titles. For expanded information, individual episode titles are hyperlinked, typically redirecting users to a partner site like TVmaze, where they can find detailed plot summaries, cast lists, and crew details [5].

For users seeking a simplified overview of a show, each series page offers special viewing formats, including a "one page" option. This condenses the entire episode list into a single, continuous page for easy reading or printing [5]. The site offers several schedule grids, most notably a Primetime grid for the five major US networks (ABC, CBS, NBC, Fox, CW), along with national television schedules for the UK, Canada, and Australia [3]. For data export, every show list can be downloaded as a tab-delimited text file by clicking the ".txt" link, allowing the information to be easily imported into spreadsheet applications [4].

As the site is not a wiki and is maintained by an editor, users are instructed to submit corrections or updates by emailing the editor directly. All submissions must be accompanied by a verifiable, reliable source [2]. Beyond its core database, the main navigation highlights several additional resources, including a detailed **FAQ** section, a **Calendar** for tracking season premiere and finale dates, and a distinct section for **Radio** show listings [1].

## References
[1] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Home Page*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/
[2] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Frequently Asked Questions*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/
[3] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Schedules*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/grid/
[4] epguides.com. (n.d.). *FAQ: Can I download your data?* Retrieved from https://epguides.com/faq/#download
[5] epguides.com. (n.d.). *Example Series Page (Doctor Who)*. Retrieved from https://epguides.com/DoctorWho_2005/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify the site as epguides.com and support it with founding year (1995), scope (12,000+ shows), and its distinctive linking to TVmaze. Both pass the core entity check.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers more checklist elements in fuller detail. A references the site’s long-running mission (“cataloging the TV universe” since 1995), mentions 12,000+ shows (closer to the 12,400+ benchmark than B’s 12,100), explains the TVmaze linking structure, describes simplified/print-friendly full-episode summaries, outlines multiple export formats (CSV, tab-delimited, text), and lists a richer set of additional navigation resources (themes, radio, merchandise, FAQ, related sites). While both miss some regional grids (e.g., NZ, JP, KR) and neither perfectly explains the TVmaze/TV.com correction pathway, B is narrower overall—mentioning fewer export formats, fewer regions, and fewer additional site sections.

Presentation & UX: Agent A uses clearer emphasis (bolded site name, structured flow) and provides denser contextual detail. B is clean but slightly thinner in scope and specificity.

Conclusion: Both are accurate, but Agent A is more comprehensive and information-dense, making it better aligned with user expectations for a rich search-style answer. Hence, A_BETTER, with B losing on WIDE completeness rather than core logic.
